purpose: Prove the mixed-pool erosion: 57 questions screened as MIXED on base, evolution after DPO training

methodology.initial_state: training_samples_v3_hard.json — first step of training, each question evaluated with 4 generations
methodology.post_training: results/Run 2/eval_finetuned_A_training.json — DPO model evaluated with 4 generations

drifted_to_all_correct.question: A baseball player has a current batting average of .250 (probability of a hit p = 0.250). In a game, he will bat four times (n = 4). This follows a binomial distribution. What is the probability of no hits?

For a certain type of airplane to fly, at least two out of its three engines must function. The engine reliability at time T hours is 0.995. Find the probability the airplane flies successfully for T hours assuming identical and independent engines.
Suppose that a population of components follows the life-distribution model: F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/2000)^{0.5}}. What proportion of new units will last more than 5000 hours?

A data center cooling system consists of 4 independent chillers arranged in a 2-out-of-4 active redundancy configuration (system succeeds if at least 2 chillers are operational). Each chiller has a time-to-failure that follows a Weibull distribution with shape parameter β = 1.5 and characteristic life η = 8000 hours. What is the reliability of a single chiller at t = 5000 hours?

A power supply unit has a lifetime that follows a lognormal distribution with a median life T₅₀ of 80,000 hours. Quality control testing reveals that 8% of units fail before 1,500 hours of operation, yielding a shape parameter σ of 2.83. Calculate the expected reliability (survival probability) at 10,000 hours of operation.

A data center uses a redundant power supply system with three independent power modules arranged in a 2-out-of-3 configuration (the system functions if at least 2 out of 3 modules are working). Each power module has a reliability of 0.92 over a one-year period. What is the reliability of the power supply system for one year?

A manufacturing quality control system uses a 3-out-of-5 redundancy configuration for its inspection sensors, meaning the system operates correctly as long as at least 3 of the 5 sensors are functioning. Each sensor has an exponentially distributed lifetime with a mean time to failure (MTTF) of 2,000 hours. All sensors operate independently and simultaneously. What is the reliability of a single sensor at t = 1,200 hours?

A telecommunications company is designing a redundant power supply system for a cell tower. The system consists of 5 independent battery backup units, each with a reliability of 0.92 for a 24-hour period during a power outage. If the tower requires at least 3 battery units to function during an outage, what is the 24-hour reliability of the power supply system?

A quality control engineer is using a single sampling plan for incoming batches of automotive sensors with acceptance number c = 1 and sample size n = 78 (determined using the Poisson approximation with AQL = 0.8% and RQL = 5.0%). What is the probability of accepting a lot that has exactly 2.5% defective items?

A power supply unit has a lifetime that follows a Weibull distribution with shape parameter β = 2.5 and scale parameter η = 5000 hours. Find the MTTF (Mean Time To Failure) for this power supply. Note: MTTF = η·Γ(1 + 1/β). Use Γ(1.4) ≈ 0.8873.

A reliability engineer is analyzing field failure data for a critical automotive component. Historical data shows that the component's lifetime follows a Weibull distribution with a characteristic life (η) of 150,000 miles and a shape parameter (β) of 2.5. Calculate the reliability (probability of survival) at 100,000 miles.

An engineer is conducting an accelerated life test on ceramic capacitors using temperature as the accelerating stress. The Arrhenius model applies with the life-stress relationship $L(T) = C \cdot e^{E_a/(k_B T)}$, where $E_a$ is the activation energy, $k_B = 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K, and $T$ is absolute temperature in Kelvin. Tests are run at two elevated temperatures. At 400 K, 20 units are tested and 15 failures are observed with a sample mean log-lifetime of $\bar{y}_1 = 6.2$ (where $y = \ln t$ in hours). At 450 K, 20 units are tested and 18 failures are observed with a sample mean log-lifetime of $\bar{y}_2 = 4.7$. The lifetime at each stress follows a lognormal distribution with common shape parameter $\sigma = 0.8$. Assuming the Arrhenius model holds, estimate the median lifetime (in hours) at the normal use condition of 340 K.

A chemical plant has a safety system consisting of two subsystems in series. Subsystem A is a 2-out-of-3 voting system where each channel has independent failures following a Weibull distribution with shape parameter β = 2.2 and scale parameter η = 8000 hours. Subsystem B is a single component whose time-to-failure follows an exponential distribution with a failure rate of λ = 0.00005 per hour. A common cause failure (CCF) can simultaneously disable all three channels of Subsystem A; the CCF event follows an exponential distribution with a rate of λ_CCF = 0.000002 per hour. Assuming Subsystem A fails if either the common cause event occurs OR fewer than 2 of the 3 channels survive (and these are independent mechanisms), and the overall system fails if either subsystem fails, what is the system reliability at t = 3000 hours?

A fleet of 10 identical turbines is placed on test. Their lifetimes follow a Weibull distribution with unknown parameters β and η. By 1000 hours, 3 turbines have failed (at times 412, 658, and 891 hours). The remaining 7 turbines are still running at 1000 hours (right-censored). The maximum likelihood estimates yield β = 2.5 and η = 1800 hours. Using these MLE parameters, a new system is designed consisting of a series arrangement of 3 independent subsystems: Subsystem A contains 2 such turbines in active parallel (system A fails when both fail), Subsystem B is a single turbine, and Subsystem C contains 3 turbines in a 2-out-of-3 configuration (subsystem C fails when at least 2 of the 3 turbines fail). Compute the system reliability at t = 1200 hours.

A chemical processing plant has a critical safety system with the following architecture: two identical primary subsystems in parallel (active redundancy), followed in series by a single backup controller. Each primary subsystem consists of a sensor and an actuator in series. The sensors have Weibull-distributed lifetimes with shape parameter β = 2.0 and scale parameter η = 8000 hours. The actuators have Weibull-distributed lifetimes with shape parameter β = 1.5 and scale parameter η = 12000 hours. The backup controller has a constant failure rate of λ = 0.0001 failures/hour. All components are independent. However, due to a shared environmental stress (common cause), there is a probability of 0.05 that both primary subsystems fail simultaneously at any given demand, modeled using the beta-factor method. Using the beta-factor common cause failure model, what is the system reliability at t = 3000 hours?

A critical safety system consists of two identical subsystems in parallel (both must fail for system failure). Each subsystem contains three components in series. Components of Type A have exponentially distributed lifetimes with a failure rate of 0.0003 per hour. Components of Type B follow a Weibull distribution with shape parameter β = 2.0 and scale parameter η = 5000 hours. Components of Type C have exponentially distributed lifetimes with a failure rate of 0.0005 per hour. Each subsystem has one Type A, one Type B, and one Type C component in series. Additionally, there is a common cause failure (CCF) that can kill both subsystems simultaneously, modeled as an exponential process with a failure rate of 0.000002 per hour. Assuming all failures are independent (except for the CCF which affects both subsystems), compute the system reliability at t = 2000 hours.

drifted_to_all_wrong.question: An engineer is performing a Bayesian reliability analysis on a new turbine blade design. The prior distribution for the failure rate λ (failures per 1000 hours) is Gamma with shape parameter α₀ = 3 and rate parameter β₀ = 2000 (so the prior mean is 0.0015 per hour). A life test is conducted on 10 identical blades. The test is time-censored at 800 hours. During the test, 4 blades fail at times 150, 310, 475, and 620 hours. The remaining 6 blades survive beyond 800 hours without failure. Assuming an exponential lifetime model, compute the Bayesian posterior mean estimate of the system's reliability at t = 500 hours, where "system" here refers to a single blade.

A fleet manager observes failure data for a critical engine component believed to follow a Weibull distribution. Six engines are put on test. Failures are observed at times 1200, 2800, and 4500 hours. The remaining three engines are still running (right-censored) at 5000 hours. Using maximum likelihood estimation, the shape parameter is estimated to be β = 2.0 (given). Using this fixed β, find the MLE of the characteristic life η (in hours), and then compute the estimated reliability of a new component at t = 3000 hours using those parameter estimates.

A chemical plant has a safety system consisting of two subsystems in series. Subsystem 1 is a 2-out-of-3 redundant configuration using identical components, each with a Weibull time-to-failure distribution with shape parameter β₁ = 2.0 and scale parameter η₁ = 8000 hours. Subsystem 2 is a single component with an exponential time-to-failure distribution with MTBF = 12000 hours, but it is supported by a cold standby spare that is identical and has a perfect sensing/switching mechanism. The standby spare only begins to age when activated. A common cause failure (CCF) can independently knock out the entire system (both subsystems simultaneously) with a constant rate of λ_CCF = 2.0 × 10⁻⁶ per hour. Assuming the common cause failure is independent of the individual component failures, compute the overall system reliability at t = 2000 hours.

A fleet of 10 identical turbine engines is placed on a reliability life test. The lifetimes are assumed to follow a Weibull distribution with a known shape parameter β=2.0. The test is terminated (Type II right censoring) after the 6th failure. The six observed failure times (in hundreds of hours) are: 12, 18, 25, 31, 40, and 52. The remaining 4 units are still operating at the time of the 6th failure (i.e., censored at t=52). Using maximum likelihood estimation for the characteristic life η, a Bayesian engineer has a prior belief that η follows a uniform distribution between 30 and 80 (in hundreds of hours). Using the MLE estimate of η as a point estimate, compute the system reliability at t=35 hundred hours for a configuration where 3 such engines are arranged in a parallel redundant system (system fails only when all 3 engines fail).

A fleet of 20 identical turbine blades is placed on a life test. The blades are believed to follow a Weibull distribution. The test is Type-II right-censored: it is terminated after the 12th failure. The first 12 ordered failure times (in hundreds of hours) are: 4.1, 5.3, 6.0, 7.2, 8.1, 8.8, 9.5, 10.4, 11.6, 12.9, 14.3, 16.0. Using maximum likelihood estimation, the shape parameter is estimated as β = 2.8 and the scale parameter as η = 14.2 (hundreds of hours). A parallel system is constructed using 3 of these blades operating independently. An engineer wants to know the system-level mean time to failure (MTTF) of this parallel configuration. Using the estimated Weibull parameters, compute the MTTF of the 3-blade parallel system in hundreds of hours, rounded to two decimal places.

A safety-critical system consists of two subsystems in series. Subsystem A is a 2-out-of-3 redundant configuration where each component follows a Weibull distribution with shape parameter β = 2 and scale parameter η = 500 hours. Subsystem B is a single component with an exponential failure distribution (constant failure rate λ = 0.0008 failures per hour) backed by a cold standby spare (identical, with a perfect switch). The standby spare only begins aging when activated. No repairs are possible. Calculate the system reliability at t = 200 hours.

A fleet manager observes failure data for a critical engine component. From prior engineering analysis, the failure times are believed to follow an exponential distribution. The prior distribution on the failure rate λ is Gamma with shape α₀ = 3 and rate β₀ = 800 (so the prior mean is 3/800 = 0.00375 failures per hour). During a test program, 8 engines are placed on test. Five engines fail at times 150, 210, 340, 425, and 580 hours. The remaining 3 engines are still running (right-censored) at 600 hours each. Using Bayesian updating with this conjugate prior, compute the posterior predictive reliability of a NEW engine at t = 400 hours — that is, the probability that a new engine from this population survives at least 400 hours, integrating over the posterior distribution of λ.
